1→# Changelog
  2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
  3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
  4→
  5→## Unreleased
  6→
  7→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
  8→
  9→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
 10→
 11→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
 12→
 13→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
 14→
 15→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
 16→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
 17→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
 18→
 19→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
 20→
 21→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
 22→
 23→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
 24→
 25→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
 26→
 27→### Added
 28→
 29→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
 30→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
 31→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
 32→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
 33→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
 34→* Wrap multiple statements on same line `wrapping` ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
 35→* Inform user about using `--format` option of KtLint CLI when finding a violation that can be autocorrected ([#1071](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1071))
 36→
 37→### Removed
 38→
 39→### Fixed
 40→
 41→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
 42→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))
 43→* Ignore property with name `serialVersionUID` in `property-naming` ([#2045](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2045))
 44→* Prevent incorrect reporting of violations in case a nullable function type reference exceeds the maximum line length `parameter-list-wrapping` ([#1324](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1324)) 
 45→* Prevent false negative on `else` branch when body contains only chained calls or binary expression ([#2057](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2057))
 46→
 47→### Changed
 48→
 49→* Fix Java interoperability issues with `RuleId` and `RuleSetId` classes. Those classes were defined as value classes in `0.49.0` and `0.49.1`. Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))
 50→* Update dependency `info.picocli:picocli` to v4.7.4
 51→* Update dependency `org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter` to v5.9.3
 52→* Update Kotlin development version to `1.8.22` and Kotlin version to `1.8.22`.
 53→* Update dependency io.github.detekt.sarif4k:sarif4k to v0.4.0
 54→* Update dependency org.jetbrains.dokka:dokka-gradle-plugin to v1.8.20
 55→* Run format up to 3 times in case formatting introduces changes which also can be autocorrected ([#2084](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2084))
 56→
 57→## [0.49.1] - 2023-05-12
 58→
 59→### Added
 60→
 61→### Removed
 62→
 63→### Fixed
 64→* Store path of file containing a lint violation relative to the location of the baseline file itself ([#1962](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1962))
 65→* Print absolute path of file in lint violations when flag "--relative" is not specified in Ktlint CLI ([#1963](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1963)) 
 66→* Handle parameter `--code-style=android_studio` in Ktlint CLI identical to deprecated parameter `--android` ([#1982](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1982))
 67→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if class without body is followed by multiple blank lines until end of file `no-consecutive-blank-lines` ([#1987](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1987))
 68→* Allow to 'unset' the `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_function_signature_rule_force_multiline_when_parameter_count_greater_or_equal_than` when using `ktlint_official` code style `function-signature` ([#1977](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1977))
 69→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if or operator at start of line is followed by dot qualified expression `indent` ([#1993](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1993))
 70→* Fix indentation of multiline parameter list in function literal `indent` ([#1976](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1976))
 71→* Restrict indentation of closing quotes to `ktlint_official` code style to keep formatting of other code styles consistent with `0.48.x` and before `indent` ([#1971](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1971))
 72→* Extract rule `no-single-line-block-comment` from `comment-wrapping` rule. The `no-single-line-block-comment` rule is added as experimental rule to the `ktlint_official` code style, but it can be enabled explicitly for the other code styles as well. ([#1980](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1980))
 73→* Clean-up unwanted logging dependencies ([#1998](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1998))
 74→* Fix directory traversal for patterns referring to paths outside of current working directory or any of it child directories ([#2002](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2002))
 75→* Prevent multiple expressions on same line separated by semicolon ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
 76→
 77→### Changed
 78→
 79→* Moved class `Baseline` from `ktlint-cli` to `ktlint-cli-reporter-baseline` so that Baseline functionality is reusable for API Consumers.
 80→
 81→## [0.49.0] - 2023-04-21
 82→
 83→WARNING: This version of KtLint contains a number of breaking changes in KtLint CLI and KtLint API. If you are using KtLint with custom ruleset jars or custom reporter jars, then those need to be upgraded before you can use them with this version of ktlint. Please contact the maintainers of those jars and ask them to upgrade a.s.a.p.
 84→
 85→All rule id's in the output of Ktlint are now prefixed with a rule set. Although KtLint currently supports standard rules to be unqualified, users are encouraged to include the rule set id in all references to rules. This includes following:
 86→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
 87→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
 88→* The `source` element in the KtLint CLI `baseline.xml` file. Regenerating this file, fixes all rule references automatically.
 89→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
 90→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
 91→
 92→### Moving experimental rules to standard rule set
 93→
 94→The `experimental` rule set has been merged with `standard` rule set. The rules which formerly were part of the `experimental` rule set are still being treated as before. The rules will only be run in case `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_experimental` is enabled or in case the rule is explicitly enabled.
 95→
 96→Note that the prefix `experimental:` has to be removed from all references to this rule. Check references in:
 97→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
 98→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
 99→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
100→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
101→
102→### Promote experimental rules to non-experimental
103→
104→The rules below have been promoted to non-experimental rules:
105→* [block-comment-initial-star-alignment](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#block-comment-initial-star-alignment)
106→* [class-naming](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#classobject-naming)
107→* [comment-wrapping](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#comment-wrapping)
108→* [function-return-type-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#function-return-type-spacing)
109→* [function-start-of-body-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#function-start-of-body-spacing)
110→* [function-type-reference-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#function-type-reference-spacing)
111→* [fun-keyword-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#fun-keyword-spacing)
112→* [kdoc-wrapping](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#kdoc-wrapping)
113→* [modifier-list-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#modifier-list-spacing)
114→* [nullable-type-spacing](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#nullable-type-spacing)
115→* [spacing-between-function-name-and-opening-parenthesis](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#spacing-between-function-name-and-opening-parenthesis)
116→* [unnecessary-parentheses-before-trailing-lambda](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/standard/#unnecessary-parenthesis-before-trailing-lambda)
117→
118→Note that this only affects users that have enabled the standard ruleset while having the experimental rules disabled.
119→
120→### API Changes & RuleSet providers & Reporter Providers
121→
122→This release is intended to be the last release before the 1.0.x release of ktlint. If all goes as planned, the 1.0.x release does not contain any new breaking changes with except of removal of functionality which is deprecated in this release.
123→
124→This release contains a lot of breaking changes which aims to improve the future maintainability of Ktlint. If you get stuck while migrating, please reach out to us by creating an issue.
125→
126→#### Experimental rules
127→
128→Rules in custom rule sets can now be marked as experimental by implementing the `Rule.Experimental` interface on the rule. Rules marked with this interface will only be executed by Ktlint if `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_experimental` is enabled or if the rule itself has been enabled explicitly.
129→
130→When using this feature, experimental rules should *not* be defined in a separate rule set as that would require a distinct rule set id. When moving a rule from an experimental rule set to a non-experimental rule set this would mean that the qualified rule id changes. For users of such rules this means that ktlint directives to suppress the rule and properties in the `.editorconfig` files have to be changed.
131→
132→#### EditorConfig
133→
134→Field `defaultAndroidValue` in class `EditorConfigProperty` has been renamed to `androidStudioCodeStyleDefaultValue`. New fields `ktlintOfficialCodeStyleDefaultValue` and `intellijIdeaCodeStyleDefaultValue` have been added. Read more about this in the section "Ktlint Official code style".
135→
136→The `.editorconfig` properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` are no longer supported. Specifying those properties in the `editorConfigOverride` or `editorConfigDefaults` result in warnings at runtime.
137→
138→#### 'Ktlint Official` code style and renaming of existing code styles
139→
140→A new code style `ktlint_official` is introduced. This code style is work in progress but will become the default code style in the `1.0` release. Please try out the new code style and provide your feedback via the [issue tracker](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues).
141→
142→This `ktlint_official` code style combines the best elements from the [Kotlin Coding conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html) and [Android's Kotlin styleguide](https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide). This code style also provides additional formatting on topics which are not (explicitly) mentioned in those conventions and style guide. But do note that this code style sometimes formats code in a way which is not accepted by the default code formatters in IntelliJ IDEA and Android Studio. The formatters of those editors produce nicely formatted code in the vast majority of cases. But in a number of edge cases, the formatting contains bugs which are waiting to be fixed for several years. The new code style formats code in a way which is compatible with the default formatting of the editors whenever possible. When using this codestyle, it is best to disable (e.g. not use) code formatting in the editor.
143→
144→The existing code styles have been renamed to make more clear what the basis of the code style is.
145→
146→The `official` code style has been renamed to `intellij_idea`. Code formatted with this code style aims to be compatible with default formatter of IntelliJ IDEA. This code style is based on [Kotlin Coding conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html). If `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_code_style` has been set to `android` then do not forget to change the value of that property to `intellij_idea`. When not set, this is still the default code style of ktlint `0.49`. Note that the default code style will be changed to `ktlint_official` in the `1.0` release.
147→
148→Code style `android` has been renamed to `android_studio`. Code formatted with this code style aims to be compatible with default formatter of Android Studio. This code style is based on [Android's Kotlin styleguide](https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide). If `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_code_style` has been set to `android` then do not forget to change the value of that property to `android_studio`.
149→
150→#### Package restructuring and class relocation
151→
152→The internal structure of the Ktlint project has been revised. The Ktlint CLI and KtLint API modules have been decoupled where possible. Modules have been restructured and renamed. See [API Overview](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/api/overview/) for more information.
153→
154→This is the last release that contains module `ktlint-core` as it had too many responsibilities. All classes in this module are relocated to other modules. Some classes have also been renamed. See tables below for details. Classes that are left behind in the `ktlint-core` module are deprecated and were kept in this version for backwards compatibility only. The `ktlint-core` module will be removed in Ktlint `0.50.x`.
155→
156→Classes below have been moved from module `ktlint-core` to the new module `ktlint-rule-engine-core`:
157→
158→| Old class/package name in `ktlint-core`                  | New class/package name in `ktlint-rule-engine-core`                               |
159→|----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
160→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig               | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig                            |
161→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.EditorConfigProperties     | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.EditorConfig                            |
162→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.OptInFeatures              | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.OptInFeatures                           |
163→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType                | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.ElementType                             |
164→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.package                    | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.ASTNodeExtension                        |
165→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.IndentConfig                   | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.IndentConfig                            |
166→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule                           | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.Rule                                    |
167→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.RuleProvider                   | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleProvider                            |
168→
169→Classes below have been moved from module `ktlint-core` to the new module `ktlint-rule-engine`:
170→
171→| Old class/package name in `ktlint-core`                  | New class/package name in `ktlint-rule-engine`                               |
172→|----------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
173→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.EditorConfigDefaults       | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigDefaults                    |
174→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.EditorConfigOverride       | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigOverride                    |
175→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.KtLintParseException       | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintParseException                    |
176→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.KtLintRuleException        | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleException                     |
177→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.KtLint                         | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleEngine                        |
178→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.LintError                      | com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.LintError                               |
179→
180→Class `com.pinterest.ktlint.core.KtLint.Code.CodeFile` has been replaced with factory method `com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.Code.fromFile`. Likewise, class `com.pinterest.ktlint.core.KtLint.Code.CodeSnippet` has been replaced with factory method `com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.Code.fromSnippet`.
181→
182→Class below has been moved from module `ktlint-core` to the new module `ktlint-cli-ruleset-core`:
183→
184→| Old class/package name in `ktlint-core`                  | New class/package name in `ktlint-cli-ruleset-core`                           |
185→|----------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
186→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.RuleSetProviderV2              | com.pinterest.ktlint.cli.ruleset.core.api.RuleSetProviderV3                   |
187→
188→
189→Class below has been moved from module `ktlint-core` to the new module `ktlint-cli-reporter-core`:
190→
191→| Old class/package name in `ktlint-core`   | New class/package name in `ktlint-cli-reporter-core`     |
192→|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
193→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.KtlintVersion   | com.pinterest.ktlint.cli.reporter.core.api.KtlintVersion |
194→
195→Class below has been moved from module `ktlint-core` to the new module `ktlint-logger`:
196→
197→| Old class/package name in `ktlint-core`               | New class/package name in `ktlint-logger`                   |
198→|-------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
199→| com.pinterest.ktlint.core.KtLintKLoggerInitializer.kt | com.pinterest.ktlint.logger.api.KtLintKLoggerInitializer.kt |
200→

conversation_history.content:
1→## Why should I use ktlint?
  2→
  3→the short answer is **Simplicity**.
  4→
  5→Spending time on configuration (and maintenance down the road) of hundred-line long style config file(s) is counter-productive. Instead of wasting your energy on something that has no business value - focus on what really matters (not debating whether to use tabs or spaces).
  6→
  7→By using ktlint you put the importance of code clarity and community conventions over personal preferences. This makes things easier for people reading your code as well as frees you from having to document and explain what style potential contributor(s) have to follow.
  8→
  9→ktlint is a single binary with both linter & formatter included. All you need is to drop it in (no need to get [overwhelmed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decision_fatigue) while choosing among [dozens of code style options](https://checkstyle.sourceforge.net/checks.html)).
 10→
 11→##  How do I enable or disable a rule?
 12→
 13→An individual rule can be enabled or disabled with a rule property. The name of the rule property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id followed by a `_` and the rule id. Examples:
 14→```editorconfig
 15→ktlint_standard_final-newline = disabled # Disables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 16→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = enabled # Enables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 17→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 18→```
 19→
 20→!!! note
 21→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.
 22→
 23→##  How do I enable or disable a rule set?
 24→
 25→All rules in a rule set can be enabled or disabled with a rule set property. The name of the rule set property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id. Examples:
 26→```editorconfig
 27→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 28→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
 29→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 30→```
 31→
 32→!!! note
 33→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
 34→
 35→## Can I have my own rules on top of ktlint?
 36→
 37→Absolutely, "no configuration" doesn't mean "no extensibility". You can add your own ruleset(s) to discover potential bugs, check for anti-patterns, etc.
 38→
 39→See [adding a custom rule set](../api/custom-rule-set/) for more information.
 40→
 41→## How do I suppress errors for a line/block/file?
 42→
 43→!!! tip
 44→    Suppressing a `ktlint` violation is meant primarily as an escape latch for the rare cases when **ktlint** is not able to produce the correct result. Please report any such instances using [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues)).
 45→
 46→To disable a specific rule you'll need the rule identifier which is displayed at the end of the lint error.
 47→
 48→An error can be suppressed using:
 49→
 50→* EOL comments
 51→* Block comments
 52→* @Suppress annotations
 53→
 54→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Suppress annotation"
 55→
 56→    ```kotlin
 57→    // Suppressing all rules for the entire file
 58→    @file:Suppress("ktlint")
 59→
 60→    // Suppress a single rule (with id 'rule-id', defined in rule set with id 'rule-set-id') from the annotated construct
 61→    @Suppress("ktlint:rule-set-id:rule-id")
 62→    class Foo {}
 63→
 64→    // Suppress multiple rules for the annotated construct
 65→    @Suppress("ktlint:standard:no-wildcard-imports", "ktlint:custom-rule-set-id:custom-rule-id")
 66→    import foo.*
 67→
 68→    // Suppress all rules for the annotated construct
 69→    @Suppress("ktlint")
 70→    import foo.*
 71→    ```
 72→=== "[:material-heart:](#) EOL comments"
 73→
 74→    ```kotlin
 75→    // Suppress a single rule for the commented line
 76→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports
 77→
 78→    // Suppress multiple rules for the commented line
 79→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_other-rule-id
 80→
 81→    // Suppress all rules for the commented line
 82→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable
 83→    ```
 84→
 85→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Block comments"
 86→
 87→    ```kotlin
 88→    // Suppress a single rule for all code between the start and end tag
 89→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 90→    import foo.*
 91→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 92→
 93→    // Suppress multiple rules for all code between the start and end tag
 94→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 95→    import foo.*
 96→    /* ktlint-enable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 97→
 98→    // Suppress all rules for all code between the start and end tag
 99→    /* ktlint-disable */
100→    import foo.*
101→    /* ktlint-enable */
102→    ```
103→
104→!!! important
105→    When using the block comments, the `ktlint-enable` directive needs to specify the exact same rule-id's and in the same order as the `ktlint-disable` directive.
106→
107→!!! warning
108→    From a consistency perspective seen, it might be best to **not** mix the (EOL/Block) comment style with the annotation style in the same project.
109→
110→## How do I globally disable a rule without `.editorconfig`?
111→
112→When using Ktlint CLI, you may pass a list of disabled rules via the `--disabled_rules` command line flag. The value is a comma separated list of rule id's that have to be disabled. The rule id must be fully qualified (e.g. must be prefixed with the rule set id). 
113→
114→
115→## Why is `.editorconfig` property `disabled_rules` deprecated and how do I resolve this?
116→
117→The `.editorconfig` properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` are deprecated as of KtLint version `0.48` and are removed in version `0.49`. Those properties contain a comma separated list of rules which are disabled. Using a comma separated list of values has some disadvantages.
118→
119→A big disadvantage is that it is not possible to override the property partially in an `.editorconfig` file in a subpackage. Another disadvantage is that it is not possible to express explicitly that a rule is enabled. Lastly, (qualified) rule ids can be 20 characters or longer, which makes a list with multiple entries hard to read.
120→
121→Starting with KtLint `0.48` entire rule sets and individual rules can be disabled / enabled with a separate property per rule (set). Examples:
122→```editorconfig
123→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
124→ktlint_standard_final-newline = enabled # Enables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
125→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
126→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = disabled # Disables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
127→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
128→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
129→```
130→
131→!!! note
132→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
133→
134→!!! note
135→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.  
136→
137→## Why is wildcard import `java.util.*` not reported by the `no-wildcard-imports` rule?
138→
139→The `no-wildcard-imports` rule forbids wildcard imports, except for imports defined in `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand`. If this property is not explicitly set, it allows wildcards imports like `java.util.*` by default to keep in sync with IntelliJ IDEA behavior.
140→
141→## Can a new toggle be added to optionally (enable/disable) format code in a particular way?
142→
143→Ktlint can be configured by enabling and disabling rules. Some rules can be configured in more details with additional `.editorconfig` properties. Regularly, a new configuration option is requested to modify behavior in existing rules.
144→
145→Ktlint is restrictive with adding additional configuration settings to customize behavior in rules. Each configuration option that Ktlint offers comes with complexity that has to be maintained by only a couple of maintainers. As of that, we cannot provide tens or even hundreds of such options.
146→
147→Less configuration options also means less discussions in teams about settings to use. Unfortunately this means that you cannot tweak Ktlint exactly to the format you prefer.
148→
149→!!! tip
150→    Any idea for a new configuration option is valuable. Please create an issue for it so that it can be considered to incorporate it in Ktlint.
151→
152→## Can I use KtLint to directly format the code I'm generating with KotlinPoet?
153→
154→Yes, it is possible to use KtLint to directly format the code generated with KotlinPoet. 
155→To do so, you must include the dependencies `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-core` and `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-ruleset-standard` in your Gradle/Maven project.
156→
157→!!! warning
158→    Do not include the dependency `com.pinterest:ktlint` as that would import the entire ktlint project including unwanted dependencies. Besides a much bigger artifact, it might also result in problems regarding logging.
159→
160→To format the output of KotlinPoet with KtLint, you can use the following snippet:
161→
162→```kotlin
163→val ruleProviders = buildSet {
164→  ServiceLoader
165→      .load(RuleSetProviderV2::class.java)
166→      .flatMapTo(this) { it.getRuleProviders() }
167→}
168→val ktLintRuleEngine = KtLintRuleEngine(
169→  ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
170→  editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults.load(EDITORCONFIG_PATH),
171→)
172→ktLintRuleEngine.format(outputDir.toPath())
173→```
174→Here, outputDir refers to the directory of the generated files by KotlinPoet, ktLintRuleEngine is an instance of KtLint rule engine.
175→
176→It is also possible to format file-by-file the output of KotlinPoet if you write your `FileSpec` to a `StringBuilder()`, instead of a `File`, 
177→and send the generated code as `String` to KtLint inside a `CodeSnippet`:
178→```kotlin
179→kotlinFile.writeText(
180→  ktLintRuleEngine.format(
181→    Code.CodeSnippet(
182→      stringBuilder.toString()
183→    )
184→  )
185→)
186→```
187→
188→# Are formatter tags respected?
189→
190→As of version `0.49.x` the formatter tags of IntelliJ IDEA are respected. By default, those formatter tags are disabled. The formatter tags can be enabled with `.editorconfig` properties below:
191→```editorconfig
192→ij_formatter_tags_enabled = true # Defaults to 'false'
193→ij_formatter_off_tag = some-custom-off-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:off'
194→ij_formatter_on_tag = some-custom-on-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:on'
195→```
196→
197→When enabled, the ktlint rule checking is disabled for all code surrounded by the formatter tags.
198→

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.logger.api.initKtLintKLogger
 4→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.Rule
 5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleProvider
 6→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.EditorConfig
 7→import mu.KotlinLogging
 8→
 9→private val LOGGER = KotlinLogging.logger {}.initKtLintKLogger()
10→
11→/**
12→ * The VisitorProvider is created for each file being scanned. As the [RuleProviderSorter] logs the order in which the
13→ * rules are executed, a singleton instance of the class is used to prevent that the logs are flooded with duplicate
14→ * log lines.
15→ */
16→private val RULE_PROVIDER_SORTER = RuleProviderSorter()
17→
18→internal class VisitorProvider(
19→    /**
20→     * The set of [RuleProvider]s to be executed. This set should not contain any [RuleProvider]s which are disabled via the [EditorConfig].
21→     */
22→    ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>,
23→    /**
24→     * Creates a new [RuleProviderSorter]. Only to be used in unit tests where the same set of rules are used with distinct
25→     * [Rule.VisitorModifier]s.
26→     */
27→    recreateRuleSorter: Boolean = false,
28→) {
29→    /**
30→     * The list of [ruleProvidersSorted] is sorted based on the [Rule.VisitorModifier] of the rules.
31→     */
32→    private val ruleProvidersSorted: List<RuleProvider> =
33→        if (recreateRuleSorter) {
34→            RuleProviderSorter()
35→        } else {
36→            RULE_PROVIDER_SORTER
37→        }.getSortedRuleProviders(ruleProviders)
38→
39→    internal fun visitor(): ((rule: Rule) -> Unit) -> Unit {
40→        if (ruleProvidersSorted.isEmpty()) {
41→            LOGGER.debug { "Skipping file as no enabled rules are found to be executed" }
42→            return { _ -> }
43→        }
44→        return { visit ->
45→            ruleProvidersSorted.forEach {
46→                visit(it.createNewRuleInstance())
47→            }
48→        }
49→    }
50→}

 40→                    """.trimIndent()
 41→                }
 42→            }
 43→        }.initKtLintKLogger()
 44→
 45→// The execution of the unit tests may not be affected by the ".editorconfig" configuration of the Ktlint project itself. So each unit test
 46→// is to be run on a KtlintTestFileSystem not having any ".editorconfig" files. This variable should not be exposed, as unit tests should
 47→// not be allowed to write any content on the file system.
 48→// As this FileSystem is not modified it is not considered to be a problem that the file system is not closed after each unit test.
 49→private val KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM = KtlintTestFileSystem()
 50→
 51→/**
 52→ * AssertJ style assertion for verifying KtLint rules. This class is intended to be used as follows:
 53→ *
 54→ * ```
 55→ * // Create an assertThat assertion for a specific rule or set of rules. If possible define it at the class level
 56→ * val someRuleAssertThat = SomeRule().assertThat()
 57→ *
 58→ * @Test
 59→ * fun `Some test`() {
 60→ *     val code = .... // Original code to be formatted
 61→ *     val formattedCode = .... // The code as it should be formatted
 62→ *     someRuleAssertThat(code)
 63→ *         .hasLintViolation(1, 1, "some-error-description")
 64→ *         .isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
 65→ * }
 66→ * ```
 67→ */
 68→@Suppress("MemberVisibilityCanBePrivate")
 69→public class KtLintAssertThat(
 70→    /**
 71→     * Provider of a rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created.
 72→     */
 73→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
 74→    /**
 75→     * The code which is to be linted and formatted.
 76→     */
 77→    private val code: String,
 78→    /**
 79→     * Providers of rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note
 80→     * that lint errors for those rules are suppressed.
 81→     */
 82→    private val additionalRuleProviders: MutableSet<RuleProvider>,
 83→    /**
 84→     * EditorConfig properties to be applied by default when linting/formatting the code.
 85→     */
 86→    private val editorConfigProperties: MutableSet<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>>,
 87→) {
 88→    private var filePath: String? = null
 89→    private var kotlinScript = false
 90→
 91→    /**
 92→     * Set the [EditorConfigOverride] properties to be used by the rule. This function can be called multiple times.
 93→     * Properties which have been set before, are silently overwritten with the new vale.
 94→     */
 95→    public fun withEditorConfigOverride(vararg properties: Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>): KtLintAssertThat {
 96→        editorConfigProperties.addAll(properties)
 97→
 98→        return this
 99→    }
100→

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleId
 4→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.lang.ASTNode
 5→
 6→internal class SuppressHandler(
 7→    private val suppressionLocator: SuppressionLocator,
 8→    private val autoCorrect: Boolean,
 9→    private val emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
10→) {
11→    fun handle(
12→        node: ASTNode,
13→        ruleId: RuleId,
14→        function: (Boolean, (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit) -> Unit,
15→    ) {
16→        val suppress =
17→            suppressionLocator(
18→                node.startOffset,
19→                ruleId,
20→            )
21→        val autoCorrect = this.autoCorrect && !suppress
22→        val emit =
23→            if (suppress) {
24→                SUPPRESS_EMIT
25→            } else {
26→                this.emit
27→            }
28→        function(autoCorrect, emit)
29→    }
30→
31→    private companion object {
32→        // Swallow violation so that it is not emitted
33→        val SUPPRESS_EMIT: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit = { _, _, _ -> }
34→    }
35→}
36→

 26→
 27→private val LOGGER = KotlinLogging.logger {}.initKtLintKLogger()
 28→
 29→internal class RuleExecutionContext private constructor(
 30→    val code: Code,
 31→    val rootNode: FileASTNode,
 32→    val ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>,
 33→    val editorConfig: EditorConfig,
 34→    val positionInTextLocator: (offset: Int) -> LineAndColumn,
 35→) {
 36→    private lateinit var suppressionLocator: SuppressionLocator
 37→
 38→    init {
 39→        rebuildSuppressionLocator()
 40→    }
 41→
 42→    fun rebuildSuppressionLocator() {
 43→        suppressionLocator = SuppressionLocatorBuilder.buildSuppressedRegionsLocator(rootNode, editorConfig)
 44→    }
 45→
 46→    fun executeRule(
 47→        rule: Rule,
 48→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
 49→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
 50→    ) {
 51→        try {
 52→            rule.startTraversalOfAST()
 53→            rule.beforeFirstNode(
 54→                // The rule get access to an EditConfig which is filtered by the properties which are actually registered as being used by
 55→                // the rule. In this way it can be forced that the rule actually registers the properties that it uses and the field becomes
 56→                // reliable to be used by for example the ".editorconfig" file generator.
 57→                // Note that also the CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY is provided because that property is needed to determine the default value of an
 58→                // EditorConfigProperty that is not explicitly defined.
 59→                editorConfig.filterBy(rule.usesEditorConfigProperties.plus(CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY)),
 60→            )
 61→            this.executeRuleOnNodeRecursively(rootNode, rule, autoCorrect, emit)
 62→            rule.afterLastNode()
 63→        } catch (e: RuleExecutionException) {
 64→            throw KtLintRuleException(
 65→                e.line,
 66→                e.col,
 67→                e.rule.ruleId.value,
 68→                """
 69→                Rule '${e.rule.ruleId.value}' throws exception in file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}' at position (${e.line}:${e.col})
 70→                   Rule maintainer: ${e.rule.about.maintainer}
 71→                   Issue tracker  : ${e.rule.about.issueTrackerUrl}
 72→                   Repository     : ${e.rule.about.repositoryUrl}
 73→                """.trimIndent(),
 74→                e.cause,
 75→            )
 76→        }
 77→    }
 78→
 79→    private fun executeRuleOnNodeRecursively(
 80→        node: ASTNode,
 81→        rule: Rule,
 82→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
 83→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
 84→    ) {
 85→        /**
 86→         * The [suppressionLocator] can be changed during each visit of node when running [KtLintRuleEngine.format]. So a new handler is to
 87→         * be built before visiting the nodes.
 88→         */
 89→        val suppressHandler = SuppressHandler(suppressionLocator, autoCorrect, emit)
 90→        if (rule.shouldContinueTraversalOfAST()) {
 91→            try {
 92→                suppressHandler.handle(node, rule.ruleId) { autoCorrect, emit ->
 93→                    rule.beforeVisitChildNodes(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 94→                }
 95→                if (rule.shouldContinueTraversalOfAST()) {
 96→                    node
 97→                        .getChildren(null)
 98→                        .forEach { childNode ->
 99→                            suppressHandler.handle(childNode, rule.ruleId) { autoCorrect, emit ->
100→                                this.executeRuleOnNodeRecursively(
101→                                    childNode,
102→                                    rule,
103→                                    autoCorrect,
104→                                    emit,
105→                                )
106→                            }
107→                        }
108→                }
109→                suppressHandler.handle(node, rule.ruleId) { autoCorrect, emit ->
110→                    rule.afterVisitChildNodes(node, autoCorrect, emit)
111→                }
112→            } catch (e: Exception) {
113→                if (autoCorrect) {
114→                    // line/col cannot be reliably mapped as exception might originate from a node not present in the
115→                    // original AST
116→                    throw RuleExecutionException(
117→                        rule,
118→                        0,
119→                        0,
120→                        // Prevent extreme long stack trace caused by recursive call and only pass root cause
121→                        e.cause ?: e,
122→                    )
123→                } else {
124→                    val (line, col) = positionInTextLocator(node.startOffset)
125→                    throw RuleExecutionException(
126→                        rule,
127→                        line,
128→                        col,
129→                        // Prevent extreme long stack trace caused by recursive call and only pass root cause
130→                        e.cause ?: e,
131→                    )
132→                }
133→            }
134→        }
135→    }
136→
137→    companion object {
138→        internal fun createRuleExecutionContext(
139→            ktLintRuleEngine: KtLintRuleEngine,
140→            code: Code,
141→        ): RuleExecutionContext {
142→            val psiFileFactory = KOTLIN_PSI_FILE_FACTORY_PROVIDER.getKotlinPsiFileFactory(ktLintRuleEngine)
143→            val normalizedText = normalizeText(code.content)
144→            val positionInTextLocator = buildPositionInTextLocator(normalizedText)
145→
146→            val psiFileName =
147→                code
148→                    .filePath
149→                    ?.pathString
150→                    ?: if (code.script) {
151→                        "File.kts"
152→                    } else {
153→                        "File.kt"
154→                    }
155→            val psiFile =
156→                psiFileFactory.createFileFromText(
157→                    psiFileName,
158→                    KotlinLanguage.INSTANCE,
159→                    normalizedText,
160→                ) as KtFile
161→            psiFile
162→                .findErrorElement()
163→                ?.let { errorElement ->
164→                    val (line, col) = positionInTextLocator(errorElement.textOffset)
165→                    throw KtLintParseException(line, col, errorElement.errorDescription)
166→                }
167→
168→            val rootNode = psiFile.node
169→
170→            val editorConfig =
171→                ktLintRuleEngine
172→                    .editorConfigLoader
173→                    .load(code.filePath)
174→                    .also {
175→                        // TODO: Remove warning below in KtLint 0.52 or later as some users skips multiple versions
176→                        it.warnIfPropertyIsObsolete("disabled_rules", "0.49")
177→                        // TODO: Remove warning below in KtLint 0.52 or later as some users skips multiple versions
178→                        it.warnIfPropertyIsObsolete("ktlint_disabled_rules", "0.49")
179→                    }
180→
181→            val ruleProviders =
182→                ktLintRuleEngine
183→                    .ruleProviders(
184→                        RuleExecutionRuleFilter(editorConfig),
185→                        RunAfterRuleFilter(),
186→                    )
187→
188→            return RuleExecutionContext(
189→                code,
190→                rootNode,
191→                ruleProviders,
192→                editorConfig,
193→                positionInTextLocator,
194→            )
195→        }
196→
197→        private fun normalizeText(text: String): String {
198→            return text
199→                .replace("\r\n", "\n")
200→                .replace("\r", "\n")
201→                .replaceFirst(UTF8_BOM, "")
202→        }
203→
204→        private fun PsiElement.findErrorElement(): PsiErrorElement? {
205→            if (this is PsiErrorElement) {
206→                return this
207→            }
208→            this.children.forEach { child ->
209→                val errorElement = child.findErrorElement()
210→                if (errorElement != null) {
211→                    return errorElement
212→                }
213→            }
214→            return null
215→        }
216→    }
217→}
218→
219→private fun EditorConfig.warnIfPropertyIsObsolete(
220→    propertyName: String,
221→    ktlintVersion: String,
222→) {
223→    if (this.contains(propertyName)) {
224→        LOGGER.warn {
225→            "Editorconfig property '$propertyName' is obsolete and is not used by KtLint starting from version $ktlintVersion. Remove " +
226→                "the property from all '.editorconfig' files."
227→        }
228→    }
229→}
230→
231→private class RuleExecutionException(
232→    val rule: Rule,
233→    val line: Int,
234→    val col: Int,
235→    override val cause: Throwable,
236→) : Throwable(cause)
237→

content.text: Now let me update the CHANGELOG to document this change:

conversation_history.name: read
conversation_history.content:
1→# Changelog
 2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
 4→
 5→## Unreleased
 6→
 7→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
 8→
 9→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
10→
11→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
12→
13→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
14→
15→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
16→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
17→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
18→
19→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
20→
21→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
22→
23→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
24→
25→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
26→
27→### Added
28→
29→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
30→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
31→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
32→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
33→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
34→* Wrap multiple statements on same line `wrapping` ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
35→* Inform user about using `--format` option of KtLint CLI when finding a violation that can be autocorrected ([#1071](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1071))
36→
37→### Removed
38→
39→### Fixed
40→
41→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
42→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))
43→* Ignore property with name `serialVersionUID` in `property-naming` ([#2045](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2045))
44→* Prevent incorrect reporting of violations in case a nullable function type reference exceeds the maximum line length `parameter-list-wrapping` ([#1324](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1324)) 
45→* Prevent false negative on `else` branch when body contains only chained calls or binary expression ([#2057](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2057))
46→
47→### Changed
48→
49→* Fix Java interoperability issues with `RuleId` and `RuleSetId` classes. Those classes were defined as value classes in `0.49.0` and `0.49.1`. Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))
50→* Update dependency `info.picocli:picocli` to v4.7.4
51→* Update dependency `org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter` to v5.9.3
52→* Update Kotlin development version to `1.8.22` and Kotlin version to `1.8.22`.
53→* Update dependency io.github.detekt.sarif4k:sarif4k to v0.4.0
54→* Update dependency org.jetbrains.dokka:dokka-gradle-plugin to v1.8.20
55→* Run format up to 3 times in case formatting introduces changes which also can be autocorrected ([#2084](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2084))
56→
57→## [0.49.1] - 2023-05-12
58→
59→### Added
60→

content.text: Now let me update the FAQ documentation:

conversation_history.name: read
conversation_history.content:
1→## Why should I use ktlint?
  2→
  3→the short answer is **Simplicity**.
  4→
  5→Spending time on configuration (and maintenance down the road) of hundred-line long style config file(s) is counter-productive. Instead of wasting your energy on something that has no business value - focus on what really matters (not debating whether to use tabs or spaces).
  6→
  7→By using ktlint you put the importance of code clarity and community conventions over personal preferences. This makes things easier for people reading your code as well as frees you from having to document and explain what style potential contributor(s) have to follow.
  8→
  9→ktlint is a single binary with both linter & formatter included. All you need is to drop it in (no need to get [overwhelmed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decision_fatigue) while choosing among [dozens of code style options](https://checkstyle.sourceforge.net/checks.html)).
 10→
 11→##  How do I enable or disable a rule?
 12→
 13→An individual rule can be enabled or disabled with a rule property. The name of the rule property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id followed by a `_` and the rule id. Examples:
 14→```editorconfig
 15→ktlint_standard_final-newline = disabled # Disables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 16→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = enabled # Enables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 17→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 18→```
 19→
 20→!!! note
 21→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.
 22→
 23→##  How do I enable or disable a rule set?
 24→
 25→All rules in a rule set can be enabled or disabled with a rule set property. The name of the rule set property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id. Examples:
 26→```editorconfig
 27→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 28→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
 29→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 30→```
 31→
 32→!!! note
 33→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
 34→
 35→## Can I have my own rules on top of ktlint?
 36→
 37→Absolutely, "no configuration" doesn't mean "no extensibility". You can add your own ruleset(s) to discover potential bugs, check for anti-patterns, etc.
 38→
 39→See [adding a custom rule set](../api/custom-rule-set/) for more information.
 40→
 41→## How do I suppress errors for a line/block/file?
 42→
 43→!!! tip
 44→    Suppressing a `ktlint` violation is meant primarily as an escape latch for the rare cases when **ktlint** is not able to produce the correct result. Please report any such instances using [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues)).
 45→
 46→To disable a specific rule you'll need the rule identifier which is displayed at the end of the lint error.
 47→
 48→An error can be suppressed using:
 49→
 50→* EOL comments
 51→* Block comments
 52→* @Suppress annotations
 53→
 54→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Suppress annotation"
 55→
 56→    ```kotlin
 57→    // Suppressing all rules for the entire file
 58→    @file:Suppress("ktlint")
 59→
 60→    // Suppress a single rule (with id 'rule-id', defined in rule set with id 'rule-set-id') from the annotated construct
 61→    @Suppress("ktlint:rule-set-id:rule-id")
 62→    class Foo {}
 63→
 64→    // Suppress multiple rules for the annotated construct
 65→    @Suppress("ktlint:standard:no-wildcard-imports", "ktlint:custom-rule-set-id:custom-rule-id")
 66→    import foo.*
 67→
 68→    // Suppress all rules for the annotated construct
 69→    @Suppress("ktlint")
 70→    import foo.*
 71→    ```
 72→=== "[:material-heart:](#) EOL comments"
 73→
 74→    ```kotlin
 75→    // Suppress a single rule for the commented line
 76→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports
 77→
 78→    // Suppress multiple rules for the commented line
 79→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_other-rule-id
 80→
 81→    // Suppress all rules for the commented line
 82→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable
 83→    ```
 84→
 85→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Block comments"
 86→
 87→    ```kotlin
 88→    // Suppress a single rule for all code between the start and end tag
 89→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 90→    import foo.*
 91→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 92→
 93→    // Suppress multiple rules for all code between the start and end tag
 94→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 95→    import foo.*
 96→    /* ktlint-enable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 97→
 98→    // Suppress all rules for all code between the start and end tag
 99→    /* ktlint-disable */
100→    import foo.*
101→    /* ktlint-enable */
102→    ```
103→
104→!!! important
105→    When using the block comments, the `ktlint-enable` directive needs to specify the exact same rule-id's and in the same order as the `ktlint-disable` directive.
106→
107→!!! warning
108→    From a consistency perspective seen, it might be best to **not** mix the (EOL/Block) comment style with the annotation style in the same project.
109→
110→## How do I globally disable a rule without `.editorconfig`?
111→
112→When using Ktlint CLI, you may pass a list of disabled rules via the `--disabled_rules` command line flag. The value is a comma separated list of rule id's that have to be disabled. The rule id must be fully qualified (e.g. must be prefixed with the rule set id). 
113→
114→
115→## Why is `.editorconfig` property `disabled_rules` deprecated and how do I resolve this?
116→
117→The `.editorconfig` properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` are deprecated as of KtLint version `0.48` and are removed in version `0.49`. Those properties contain a comma separated list of rules which are disabled. Using a comma separated list of values has some disadvantages.
118→
119→A big disadvantage is that it is not possible to override the property partially in an `.editorconfig` file in a subpackage. Another disadvantage is that it is not possible to express explicitly that a rule is enabled. Lastly, (qualified) rule ids can be 20 characters or longer, which makes a list with multiple entries hard to read.
120→
121→Starting with KtLint `0.48` entire rule sets and individual rules can be disabled / enabled with a separate property per rule (set). Examples:
122→```editorconfig
123→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
124→ktlint_standard_final-newline = enabled # Enables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
125→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
126→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = disabled # Disables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
127→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
128→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
129→```
130→
131→!!! note
132→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
133→
134→!!! note
135→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.  
136→
137→## Why is wildcard import `java.util.*` not reported by the `no-wildcard-imports` rule?
138→
139→The `no-wildcard-imports` rule forbids wildcard imports, except for imports defined in `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand`. If this property is not explicitly set, it allows wildcards imports like `java.util.*` by default to keep in sync with IntelliJ IDEA behavior.
140→
141→## Can a new toggle be added to optionally (enable/disable) format code in a particular way?
142→
143→Ktlint can be configured by enabling and disabling rules. Some rules can be configured in more details with additional `.editorconfig` properties. Regularly, a new configuration option is requested to modify behavior in existing rules.
144→
145→Ktlint is restrictive with adding additional configuration settings to customize behavior in rules. Each configuration option that Ktlint offers comes with complexity that has to be maintained by only a couple of maintainers. As of that, we cannot provide tens or even hundreds of such options.
146→
147→Less configuration options also means less discussions in teams about settings to use. Unfortunately this means that you cannot tweak Ktlint exactly to the format you prefer.
148→
149→!!! tip
150→    Any idea for a new configuration option is valuable. Please create an issue for it so that it can be considered to incorporate it in Ktlint.
151→
152→## Can I use KtLint to directly format the code I'm generating with KotlinPoet?
153→
154→Yes, it is possible to use KtLint to directly format the code generated with KotlinPoet. 
155→To do so, you must include the dependencies `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-core` and `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-ruleset-standard` in your Gradle/Maven project.
156→
157→!!! warning
158→    Do not include the dependency `com.pinterest:ktlint` as that would import the entire ktlint project including unwanted dependencies. Besides a much bigger artifact, it might also result in problems regarding logging.
159→
160→To format the output of KotlinPoet with KtLint, you can use the following snippet:
161→
162→```kotlin
163→val ruleProviders = buildSet {
164→  ServiceLoader
165→      .load(RuleSetProviderV2::class.java)
166→      .flatMapTo(this) { it.getRuleProviders() }
167→}
168→val ktLintRuleEngine = KtLintRuleEngine(
169→  ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
170→  editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults.load(EDITORCONFIG_PATH),
171→)
172→ktLintRuleEngine.format(outputDir.toPath())
173→```
174→Here, outputDir refers to the directory of the generated files by KotlinPoet, ktLintRuleEngine is an instance of KtLint rule engine.
175→
176→It is also possible to format file-by-file the output of KotlinPoet if you write your `FileSpec` to a `StringBuilder()`, instead of a `File`, 
177→and send the generated code as `String` to KtLint inside a `CodeSnippet`:
178→```kotlin
179→kotlinFile.writeText(
180→  ktLintRuleEngine.format(
181→    Code.CodeSnippet(
182→      stringBuilder.toString()
183→    )
184→  )
185→)
186→```
187→
188→# Are formatter tags respected?
189→
190→As of version `0.49.x` the formatter tags of IntelliJ IDEA are respected. By default, those formatter tags are disabled. The formatter tags can be enabled with `.editorconfig` properties below:
191→```editorconfig
192→ij_formatter_tags_enabled = true # Defaults to 'false'
193→ij_formatter_off_tag = some-custom-off-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:off'
194→ij_formatter_on_tag = some-custom-on-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:on'
195→```
196→
197→When enabled, the ktlint rule checking is disabled for all code surrounded by the formatter tags.
198→

content.thinking:
The existing tests still test the suppression using `ktlint-disable` directives. These tests should still pass because:
1. The `SuppressionLocatorBuilder` still processes `ktlint-disable` directives for suppression (we didn't remove that functionality)
2. The new `KtlintSuppressionRule` will report violations for using those deprecated directives, but these test classes don't include the `KtlintSuppressionRule` rule provider

However, I notice that since we inject the `KtlintSuppressionRule` via the `InternalRuleProvidersFilter`, the existing tests may now also report violations from this rule. Let me check this by looking at how we inject the internal rule providers and whether this will affect the existing tests.

Looking at my implementation:
1. `InternalRuleProvidersFilter` is applied in `RuleExecutionContext.createRuleExecutionContext()`
2. This means every time `KtLintRuleEngine.lint()` is called, the `KtlintSuppressionRule` will be included

So the tests in `SuppressionLocatorBuilderTest` will now also report violations from the `KtlintSuppressionRule`. These tests use `assertThat(lint(code)).isEmpty()` or `assertThat(lint(code)).containsExactly(...)`, so they will fail because they now also contain violations from the `KtlintSuppressionRule`.

I need to update these tests to filter out the violations from the `KtlintSuppressionRule`, or I need to reconsider the design.

Actually, looking more carefully at the implementation, I think the better approach is to:
1. Not automatically inject the `KtlintSuppressionRule` into every lint run
2. Instead, only emit warnings when the deprecated directives are used

This makes more sense because:
1. It's a warning about deprecation, not a hard error
2. The old behavior should continue to work (suppression should still work)


3. Developers can gradually transition their codebase, allowing for a smooth migration path that doesn't disrupt existing projects. This approach provides flexibility for teams with large codebases or complex legacy systems, enabling them to update their suppression mechanisms incrementally without causing immediate breaking changes.

The recommendation focuses on a pragmatic strategy that balances deprecation with practical implementation. By offering an optional rule and maintaining warning mechanisms, the approach ensures developers are informed about outdated practices while having the autonomy to update their code at their own pace.
